info.name: YoUnion — Supabase PostgREST
info.description:
Read-focused collection for inspecting YoUnion database tables via Supabase's auto-generated PostgREST API.

## Setup
1. Import `younion.postman_environment.json` alongside this collection.
2. Set `supabase_url` (e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:54321` for local, your project URL for hosted).
3. Set `supabase_key` to your **publishable** key (`SUPABASE_KEY` in `.env`). This grants anon access — RLS-protected tables (users, user_profiles, user_cost_of_living) will return `[]` until you call **Auth → Sign in with password**, which captures a user JWT into `{{user_jwt}}` for you.
4. Open any folder and hit Send.

## Headers
- `apikey`: identifies the project. Always the publishable key.
- `Authorization: Bearer ...`: anon access if it's the publishable key, authenticated access if it's a user JWT.

The collection-level `Authorization` defaults to `{{supabase_key}}` so anon-readable tables Just Work. To switch to authenticated mode, change a request's auth to `Bearer {{user_jwt}}` (or override at the folder level).

## Query DSL cheatsheet (PostgREST)
- `?select=col1,col2` — projection
- `?col=eq.value` — equality (`gt`, `lt`, `gte`, `lte`, `neq`, `like`, `ilike`, `in.(a,b)`, `is.null`)
- `?order=col.desc.nullslast`
- `?limit=25&offset=50`
- `?select=*,related_table(*)` — embed FK relations
- Header `Prefer: count=exact` — total row count in `Content-Range` response header
- Header `Accept: application/vnd.pgrst.object+json` — return single object instead of array (404 if 0 or >1 rows)

item.name: 00 — Start Here (Learn by Querying)
item.description:
A guided tour of querying Supabase via Postman. Each request is hittable against your real DB and teaches one concept. Run them in order.

## What 'Supabase via Postman' really is
Supabase auto-exposes every Postgres table as a REST endpoint via PostgREST: `GET https://<project>.supabase.co/rest/v1/<table_name>?<query>`.

The SQL `WHERE`, `SELECT`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`, etc. are translated into URL query parameters with a small DSL:
- Filters: `column=op.value` (e.g. `?ticker=eq.AAPL`)
- Projection: `select=col1,col2`
- Ordering: `order=col.desc`
- Pagination: `limit=N&offset=M`

That's the whole game. Everything else is variations.

## How to use this folder
Go top to bottom. Hit Send on each one. Read the description in the right panel BEFORE clicking Send so you know what to look for in the response. Then look at each row in the **Params** and **Headers** tabs — every row has its own description (hover or click).

## Glossary of terms you'll meet
- **PostgREST**: the open-source layer that turns Postgres tables into a REST API. Supabase wraps it.
- **DSL**: domain-specific language — here it just means PostgREST's query-string syntax.
- **jsonb**: Postgres's binary JSON type. Several columns in this DB (raw_data, ai_summary, committees) are jsonb.
- **Embed**: PostgREST's term for joining a related table into the response.
- **RLS**: row-level security — rows can be hidden from anon callers. Sign in to see them.

item.name: Lesson 01 — Read everything (with a safety limit)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT * FROM companies LIMIT 5;`

**What you're looking at**
- The response is a JSON array, one element per row.
- `select=*` means "all columns". You'll see id (uuid), ticker, name, cik, sector, industry, exchange, last_fetch_at, created_at, updated_at.
- `limit=5` caps the result. Without it, you'd get every company in the DB. **Always start with a limit** when exploring an unfamiliar table.

**Try this**
- Change `limit` to `1`. Notice the response is still an array (`[{ ... }]`), just shorter.
- Change `limit` to `100`. Watch how long it takes.
- Remove `limit` entirely. Read the warning about why this is a bad idea on big tables.

**Concept**
Every PostgREST request returns an array unless you opt into single-object mode (you'll learn that in Lesson 04).

header.description: Your Supabase publishable key. Identifies the project. Required on every request.

query.description: Project all columns. The asterisk is interpreted by PostgREST, not the URL — no escaping needed.
Max rows to return. Defaults to no limit. ALWAYS set this when exploring a new table.

item.name: Lesson 02 — Pick specific columns (projection)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT ticker, name, sector FROM companies LIMIT 5;`

**What you're looking at**
Returning every column is wasteful when you only need three. Projection trims the response.

**Why this matters for precision**
Many tables in YoUnion have fat jsonb columns:
- `filing_summaries.raw_data` — verbatim SEC API JSON, can be megabytes
- `filing_summaries.ai_summary` — Claude-generated summary blob
- `companies` is small, but the same principle applies everywhere

Projecting only the columns you need is the single biggest performance win in PostgREST.

**Try this**
- Add `created_at` to the select list. Format: comma-separated, no spaces.
- Try `select=*,sector` — yes, you can mix `*` with extra fields (rarely useful, but legal).
- Try `select=name:legal_name` to alias a column. The response will have a key `legal_name` instead of `name`. Useful when consuming the response programmatically.

query.description: Comma-separated column list. No spaces between names. To rename a column on output: `output_name:db_column`.

item.name: Lesson 03 — Filter to specific rows (WHERE clause)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT ticker, name FROM companies WHERE ticker = 'AAPL';`

**What you're looking at**
A filter is just `column=op.value` in the URL. The operator is the *prefix* before the dot.

This request returns an array — even if only one row matches — because that's the default shape. Lesson 04 shows you how to ask for an object.

**The full operator menu**
| Operator | Meaning | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `eq` | equals | `ticker=eq.AAPL` |
| `neq` | not equal | `ticker=neq.AAPL` |
| `gt`/`gte` | greater than (or equal) | `cik=gt.1000000` |
| `lt`/`lte` | less than (or equal) | `cik=lt.9999999` |
| `like`/`ilike` | SQL LIKE / case-insensitive (`*` is wildcard) | `name=ilike.*apple*` |
| `in` | value in list | `ticker=in.(AAPL,MSFT,GOOGL)` |
| `is` | IS NULL / IS TRUE / IS FALSE | `sector=is.null` |

**Try this**
- Change to `ticker=eq.MSFT`. Then `ticker=eq.NONEXISTENT` — note the empty array `[]`, not an error.
- Try `sector=eq.Technology` to filter by sector.
- Try `name=ilike.*apple*` for substring search (case-insensitive).
- Multiple filters AND together: `&sector=eq.Technology&exchange=eq.NASDAQ`.

query.description: Filter where ticker equals 'AAPL'. The format is always `column=operator.value`.

item.name: Lesson 04 — Get one row as an object (not array)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent (sort of):** `SELECT ... LIMIT 1` plus client-side `[0]`.

**What you're looking at**
The `Accept: application/vnd.pgrst.object+json` header tells PostgREST: "I want a single object back, not an array." If 0 or 2+ rows match, you get HTTP 406, not a successful empty response. So this is for when you *expect* exactly one row.

**Why this matters for precision**
When you're extracting one specific company's name, you don't want to write `response[0].name` — you want `response.name`. This header makes that work.

**Try this**
- Compare the response shape to Lesson 03. Same data, different wrapper.
- Change to `ticker=eq.NONEXISTENT`. Note the 406 status — that's PostgREST telling you the row doesn't exist.
- Combine with column projection: `select=name`. Response is now `{"name": "Apple Inc."}`. You're one JSON parse away from a scalar.

header.description: Strip the array wrapper. Returns a single object. 406 if rows matched != 1.

item.name: Lesson 05 — Extract a single value (scalar drill-down)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT name FROM companies WHERE ticker = 'AAPL';` → returns `"Apple Inc."`

**What you're looking at**
Combine the three techniques you just learned:
1. **Filter** to one row (`ticker=eq.AAPL`)
2. **Project** one column (`select=name`)
3. **Single object** (Accept header)

Response: `{"name": "Apple Inc."}`. The actual scalar is `.name`.

**This is the precision pattern.** Whenever you want "the X of company Y", build it like this. Some examples to try:
- `companies?ticker=eq.AAPL&select=cik` → CIK number
- `companies?ticker=eq.AAPL&select=sector` → sector string
- `executive_compensation?company_id=eq.{{company_id}}&fiscal_year=eq.2024&title=ilike.*CEO*&select=executive_name,total_compensation&order=total_compensation.desc&limit=1` → CEO name + comp

**Note**
PostgREST has no built-in way to return *just* the bare string — you always get a JSON object/array. To go scalar, parse client-side.

query.description: Narrow to one row.
Project one column. Response shape: {"name": "..."}.

item.name: Lesson 06 — Drill into a jsonb column (sub-field extraction)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT raw_data->'formType' FROM filing_summaries WHERE company_id = '...' AND filing_type = '10-K' ORDER BY filed_at DESC LIMIT 1;`

**What you're looking at**
Most interesting data in YoUnion lives inside jsonb columns:
- `filing_summaries.raw_data` — the verbatim SEC API JSON for a filing
- `filing_summaries.ai_summary` — Claude's structured summary
- `directors.committees`, `directors.qualifications` — list-shaped metadata

PostgREST exposes Postgres's jsonb arrow operators directly in the select clause:
- `column->key` — returns the value as **jsonb** (preserves nested structure)
- `column->>key` — returns the value as **text** (stringifies it)
- `column->idx` — for jsonb arrays (numeric index)
- `column#>'{a,b,c}'` — path drill (jsonb)
- `column#>>'{a,b,c}'` — path drill (text)

**Why this matters for precision**
Instead of pulling the entire 5MB `raw_data` blob and parsing it client-side, you can ask the database to extract just the field you want. The wire payload shrinks dramatically.

**Try this**
- This request pulls only `raw_data->>'formType'` for the latest 10-K.
- Edit the select to `raw_data->>'cik'` to get the CIK from inside the SEC JSON.
- Try `ai_summary->>'summary'` (if AI summary is populated) to pull the summary text.
- Use `value:raw_data->>'formType'` to alias the output field to `value`.

**Requires `{{company_id}}` in your environment.** Run a Lesson 03 query against `companies` first, then copy an `id` into the env.

query.description: Set this env var first by running a companies query and copying an id.
Annual report. Other values: 10-Q (quarterly), 8-K (current event), DEF 14A (proxy).
Pull two regular columns plus one extracted from inside the raw_data jsonb. `->>` returns text; `->` would return jsonb.

item.name: Lesson 07 — Filter precisely with comparison operators
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT executive_name, title, total_compensation FROM executive_compensation WHERE fiscal_year >= 2022 AND total_compensation >= 10000000 ORDER BY total_compensation DESC LIMIT 25;`

**What you're looking at**
Multiple top-level filters AND together. Each is a separate `column=op.value` query parameter. Order doesn't matter.

This pulls execs paid $10M+ since 2022, ranked by total comp.

**Try this**
- Bump the floor to `total_compensation=gte.50000000` for the top earners only.
- Add `&title=ilike.*CEO*` to scope to CEOs.
- Negate: `&title=not.ilike.*CEO*` to exclude CEOs.
- OR groups (rather than AND): `or=(title.ilike.*CEO*,title.ilike.*CFO*)`.

**Edge cases**
- `is.null` for nulls, NOT `eq.null` (which would look for the literal string 'null').
- For `in`: `?ticker=in.(AAPL,MSFT,GOOGL)` — parens required, comma-separated.
- Strings with commas: quote them — `in.("foo,bar",baz)`.

query.description: Greater-than-or-equal. Use gt/gte/lt/lte for ranges.
Stored in dollars (bigint). Bump this to narrow further.
Note `companies(ticker)` — that's a foreign-key embed. Lesson 09 covers this.
Push nulls to the bottom. `nullsfirst` is the alternative.

item.name: Lesson 08 — Sort and paginate
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT ticker, name FROM companies ORDER BY ticker ASC LIMIT 25 OFFSET 0;`

**What you're looking at**
Two ways to paginate:
- `limit=25&offset=50` — query-param style (this lesson)
- `Range: 50-74` header — Range-header style (PostgREST-native)

Both work. The Range header has one extra trick: pair it with `Prefer: count=exact` and PostgREST will return the total in the `Content-Range` response header (you'll see `0-24/1234` in the headers tab of the response). Cheap pagination metadata.

**Try this**
- Change `offset` to `25`, `50`, etc. to walk through pages.
- Change `order` to `created_at.desc` to see newest companies first.
- Multi-column sort: `order=sector.asc,ticker.asc`.
- Add the `Prefer: count=exact` header (uncomment in headers tab) and check the response headers for `Content-Range`.

header.description: Enable to get total row count in the `Content-Range` response header. Slow on huge tables.

query.description: Direction defaults to asc if omitted. Add `.nullsfirst` or `.nullslast` to control null placement.
Page 0. Increment by `limit` to walk through results.

item.name: Lesson 09 — Join via embed (related tables in one query)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT d.name, d.title, c.ticker, c.name AS company_name FROM directors d JOIN companies c ON c.id = d.company_id LIMIT 10;`

**What you're looking at**
PostgREST 'embeds' related rows into the response by detecting foreign-key relationships from your schema. Drizzle's `.references(() => companies.id)` gives PostgREST the FK metadata it needs — you don't have to do anything else.

The syntax is `select=...,related_table_name(column_list)`. Examples:
- `select=name,title,companies(ticker,name)` — pull the parent company alongside each director
- `select=*,directors(*)` — get a company AND all its directors in one round trip
- `select=*,directors(*),executive_compensation(*)` — multiple embeds at once

You can even apply filters/orders/limits *inside* the embed:
- `directors.title=eq.CEO` — filter embedded directors
- `directors.order=name.asc` — order embedded directors
- `directors.limit=5` — cap embedded rows per parent

**Why this matters for precision**
One embed = one round trip = no N+1 query problem. If you want a company plus its top 3 paid execs plus its directors, you can do it all in a single GET.

**Try this**
- Flip the direction: `companies?select=*,directors(*),executive_compensation(*)&ticker=eq.AAPL` — Apple plus everything related.
- Filter inside the embed: `companies?select=name,executive_compensation(executive_name,title,total_compensation)&executive_compensation.fiscal_year=eq.2024&executive_compensation.order=total_compensation.desc&executive_compensation.limit=3&ticker=eq.AAPL`.

query.description: The `companies(...)` part is a foreign-key embed. PostgREST detects the FK from `directors.company_id` and joins automatically.

item.name: Lesson 10 — Count rows (and what PostgREST CAN'T do)
item.description:
**SQL equivalent:** `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM companies;`

**What you're looking at**
PostgREST has no `count(*)` selector — instead, send `Prefer: count=exact` and read the **response header** `Content-Range`. The body is still rows; the header carries the total.

With `limit=0` you get the count *without* fetching any rows — purely a metadata query.

Variants:
- `count=exact` — accurate but full-scan; slow on huge tables
- `count=planned` — fast estimate from the planner
- `count=estimated` — exact when small, planned when large (good default)

**What PostgREST does NOT support**
These are deliberate omissions; for them you need a Postgres view or a SQL function exposed as RPC.
- `SELECT DISTINCT`
- `GROUP BY` with aggregates (`SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT(DISTINCT col)`)
- Window functions
- `HAVING`
- Arbitrary joins (only FK-defined relationships)

**The escape hatch**
Write a Postgres function:
```sql
CREATE FUNCTION top_paid_execs(year int, lim int)
RETURNS TABLE(executive_name text, total_comp bigint) AS $$
  SELECT executive_name, total_compensation
  FROM executive_compensation
  WHERE fiscal_year = year
  ORDER BY total_compensation DESC
  LIMIT lim;
$$ LANGUAGE sql STABLE;
```
Then call it from PostgREST: `POST /rest/v1/rpc/top_paid_execs` with body `{"year": 2024, "lim": 10}`. RPC is how you escape the table-CRUD model for anything more complex.

**Try this**
- Run as-is. Check the response **Headers** tab for `Content-Range: 0-0/<TOTAL>`.
- Combine with a filter: `?sector=eq.Technology` to count tech companies.

header.description: Tell PostgREST to compute and return the total row count. Look for `Content-Range` in the response headers.

query.description: Return zero rows — we only care about the count in the response header. Saves wire bytes.

item.name: Lesson 11 — Putting it all together (precise extraction)
item.description:
**The goal:** for a given company, get the latest 10-K's CEO compensation row + just the named executive's name and total comp.

**The query**
This combines:
1. **Filter** by company (FK), filing context, executive role
2. **Embed** the parent company name in one round trip
3. **Project** only the columns we want
4. **Order + limit** to take the highest-paid match (most likely the CEO)
5. **Single-object Accept** so the response is the row, not `[row]`

**Read this query out loud, slowly**
`/executive_compensation?company_id=eq.{{company_id}}&title=ilike.*CEO*&fiscal_year=eq.2024&select=executive_name,title,total_compensation,companies(ticker,name)&order=total_compensation.desc&limit=1`

→ "From `executive_compensation`, where `company_id` equals my env var AND `title` looks like 'CEO' (case-insensitive) AND `fiscal_year` is 2024, give me only `executive_name`, `title`, `total_compensation`, and the parent company's `ticker` and `name`, sorted by `total_compensation` descending, take the top 1, and return as a single object."

If you can compose that query without looking it up, you've internalized PostgREST.

**Try this**
- Drop the `title` filter — get the highest-paid exec regardless of title.
- Change `fiscal_year` to a different year.
- Try `select=executive_name,total_compensation,salary,bonus,stock_awards,option_awards` to see compensation breakdown.

query.description: Case-insensitive substring. Catches 'Chief Executive Officer', 'CEO', etc.
Adjust per company's available years. Pull distinct years from the table first if unsure.

item.name: 01 — Dashboard UI → Query Map
item.description:
Every panel on the company detail page is backed by a query. This folder maps each UI surface to the exact PostgREST request that produces the data behind it.

**How to use this folder**
When you see a value on the company dashboard and ask "where does that number come from?", find the matching request here. Set `{{company_id}}` in your environment to a real UUID (run `companies → By ticker` first), and every request below will work against that company.

The Edge Function source of truth for the dashboard is [supabase/functions/company-detail/index.ts](../../supabase/functions/company-detail/index.ts) — these requests reproduce its individual queries directly against PostgREST, skipping the function. That's exactly what you want when debugging or extracting precise values: bypass the assembled response, ask Postgres directly.

**Glossary**
- *Company header* = the top of the page (ticker, name, industry).
- *Leadership* = the table of named executive officers with comp.
- *CEO Spotlight* = the highlighted top-paid exec card.
- *Filings list* = recent 10-K / 10-Q / 8-K / DEF 14A items.
- *Recent events* = 8-K-derived event cards.
- *AI Summary fields* = the structured summary panels (MD&A, risk factors, what-this-means).

item.name: Company header — ticker, name, sector, exchange
item.description:
**UI surface:** the page title and metadata strip at the top of the company detail page.

**Query shape:** filter by ticker, return as a single object (because there's exactly one match per ticker — `ticker` is a unique column).

**To narrow:** add more columns to `select`, e.g. `&select=*` to see everything stored about this company.
**To broaden:** swap to `companies?sector=eq.Technology` to see all companies in a sector.

query.description: Change to any ticker. Tickers are stored uppercase.
Note `id` is the UUID you need for {{company_id}}. The `last_fetch_at` shows the freshness of SEC data.

item.name: Available fiscal years dropdown
item.description:
**UI surface:** the fiscal-year selector on the leadership panel.

**The Edge Function** uses `selectDistinct({ year })` from Drizzle. PostgREST has no DISTINCT — so the closest equivalent is to project + order, then dedupe client-side, OR write a Postgres function exposed as RPC.

This request uses the *project + order* approach: get every fiscal_year row sorted desc; the consumer dedupes. For a company with 5 execs over 3 years, you'll get 15 rows where you only need 3 distinct values. That's the price of skipping RPC.

**The clean way** (for production code): create a SQL function in Supabase like `get_fiscal_years(company_uuid)` and call it via `POST /rest/v1/rpc/get_fiscal_years`.

**To narrow:** there's nothing to narrow — distinct values are the entire purpose.
**To broaden:** drop the `company_id` filter to see fiscal years across all companies.

query.description: Project just the year. The response is an array with duplicates — dedupe client-side.
Newest first, matching the dashboard's default selection.

item.name: Leadership table (top 20 by comp, fiscal year)
item.description:
**UI surface:** the leadership/executive compensation table on the company detail page.

**The Edge Function** runs this exact pattern: filter by company + selected fiscal year, sort by total compensation desc, cap at 20.

**To narrow:** add `&title=ilike.*CEO*` to scope to one role.
**To broaden:** drop `fiscal_year` to see all years for this company. Or drop `company_id` to compare execs across the entire DB (warning — large response).

query.description: Adjust to a year that exists for this company (use the previous request to discover available years).
Returns full comp breakdown: salary, bonus, stock_awards, option_awards, non_equity_incentive, other_compensation, ceo_pay_ratio, total_compensation.

item.name: CEO Spotlight (top-paid exec)
item.description:
**UI surface:** the highlighted CEO/top-exec card on the company dashboard.

Not a separate Edge Function query — derived from the leadership data by taking the highest-paid exec. We can do that directly in PostgREST with `limit=1`.

**Note on title matching:** SEC filings use varied titles ("Chief Executive Officer", "CEO and Chairman", "President & CEO"). `title=ilike.*CEO*` catches all common variants.

**To narrow:** drop the `title` filter — get the top-paid person regardless of role.
**To broaden:** raise `limit=5` to see the C-suite top 5.

query.description: Substring, case-insensitive. Catches multiple title variants.
All comp components — useful if the UI shows a breakdown bar/donut.

item.name: Directors list
item.description:
**UI surface:** the board-of-directors panel.

No fiscal-year filter — directors are stored as a single set per company (the most recent extraction). If a director left the board, they may still appear; check `tenure_start`.

**To narrow:** `&is_independent=is.true` for independent directors only. Or `&title=ilike.*Chair*` for chair/co-chair.
**To broaden:** drop the company filter to see all directors across the DB.

query.description: `committees` is jsonb — array of committee names. Use jsonb arrows to drill in if needed.

item.name: All filings list (metadata only)
item.description:
**UI surface:** the filings timeline / list view.

The Edge Function projects only metadata — never the bulky `raw_data` jsonb. Replicate that here. If you want to inspect a single filing's body, use the next request and pass the `id`.

**To narrow:** add `&filing_type=eq.10-K` to scope to one type.
**To broaden:** drop `company_id` for cross-company filings (large response).

query.description: Deliberately excludes raw_data, ai_summary, human_summary — those can be megabytes each.

item.name: Latest 10-K (annual report) — full row
item.description:
**UI surface:** "Latest annual report" card and its drill-in screen.

**The Edge Function** finds the first 10-K with a non-null `ai_summary` after sorting by `filed_at` desc.

**To narrow further:** project specific ai_summary jsonb sub-fields with `select=ai_summary->>summary_text` (replace path with actual key).
**To broaden:** swap `eq.10-K` to `in.(10-K,10-K/A)` to include amendments.

query.description: Only filings that have been processed through AI summarization. The `not.` prefix negates `is.null`.

item.name: Latest 10-Q (quarterly)
item.description:
**UI surface:** "Latest quarterly" card.

Identical pattern to 10-K, just a different filing_type. Worth understanding as a template — every "latest X" card on the page is the same shape with a different `filing_type`.

item.name: Latest DEF 14A (proxy statement)
item.description:
**UI surface:** "Proxy statement" / compensation-disclosure related cards.

DEF 14A filings carry the executive compensation disclosure that powers the leadership table. The proxy is the source of `executive_compensation` rows.

query.description: The space in 'DEF 14A' is significant — Postman URL-encodes it automatically. If you copy the curl manually, that becomes `DEF%2014A`.

item.name: Recent 8-K events (top 5)
item.description:
**UI surface:** the "recent events" card stack on the dashboard.

Note: there are TWO sources of 8-K data:
- `filing_summaries` rows where `filing_type=eq.8-K` — the AI-summarized version
- `form_8k_events` table — parsed item-level events (next request)

This request gives the AI summary side. Use the next one for structured event types.

item.name: Form 8-K parsed events (item-level)
item.description:
**UI surface:** the structured 8-K event list (where each row carries an item type like 'Item 5.02 — Departure of Directors').

This pulls from the dedicated `form_8k_events` table, which has one row per (filing, item type). Useful when you want to filter by event category — `item_type=eq.5.02` for executive departures, etc.

**To narrow:** `&item_type=eq.5.02` for executive officer changes, `&item_type=eq.7.01` for Reg FD disclosures.
**To broaden:** drop `company_id` for cross-company event sweeps.

item.name: Insider trades (recent transactions)
item.description:
**UI surface:** the insider trading activity table.

Form 4 filings — when officers/directors buy or sell stock.

**To narrow:**
- `&transaction_type=eq.S` for sales only
- `&filer_name=ilike.*Tim Cook*` for one named insider
- `&total_value=gte.1000000` for trades over $1M

**To broaden:** raise the limit, or drop `company_id`.

query.description: transaction_type values per Form 4: 'P'=purchase, 'S'=sale, 'A'=grant/award, 'M'=option exercise, etc.

item.name: Drill into ai_summary jsonb (precise field extraction)
item.description:
**UI surface:** any individual panel inside an AI summary card — MD&A, risk factors, what-this-means, etc. — when you only want one piece of the structured response.

**The `ai_summary` jsonb shape** depends on the prompt that produced it. Look at [packages/ai/src/prompts/](../../packages/ai/src/prompts/) for the actual schemas — `company-summary.ts`, `mda-summary.ts`, `employee-impact.ts`, etc. each define a different output shape.

**The pattern**
Use jsonb arrow operators inside the `select` clause to extract specific fields without dragging the whole blob over the wire:
- `select=summary:ai_summary->>summary` — text leaf
- `select=highlights:ai_summary->highlights` — keep as jsonb (probably an array)
- `select=sub:ai_summary#>>'{section,subsection,field}'` — deep path drill

**Replace `your_field`** with the actual key you want. If you don't know the keys, run the previous request (latest 10-K full row) once, look at `ai_summary` in the response, then come back here and target the specific key.

query.description: Replace `your_field` with the actual key inside ai_summary. Use `->` for jsonb (preserves nesting), `->>` for text. The `extracted:` prefix renames the output column.

item.name: Drill into raw_data jsonb (verbatim SEC fields)
item.description:
**UI surface:** any panel showing raw SEC data — XBRL financial tables, period-end metadata, original form fields.

**The `raw_data` shape** is whatever sec-api.io returned. Common top-level keys depending on filing type: `formType`, `periodOfReport`, `cik`, `companyName`, `xbrl` (heavy), `text` (heavy).

This request shows the precision pattern: pull just `formType` and `periodOfReport` from inside the verbatim SEC blob, without dragging the megabytes of XBRL.

**To explore the shape first:** run filing_summaries → 'Latest filing — raw_data only' once to see what keys exist for this filing type, then come back and project specific paths.

query.description: Three jsonb extractions in one select. Each uses `->>` because we want strings. Each is aliased so the response keys are clean.

item.name: Composite — company + execs + directors in one round trip
item.description:
**UI surface:** the entire 'who runs this company' section, fetched in one request.

This is the embed flex. Instead of 3 separate queries (companies, executive_compensation, directors), embed both relations into the company query. PostgREST detects the FKs from the schema and joins for you.

This is also exactly what you'd build if you were rewriting the company-detail Edge Function as a single PostgREST call instead of 4 parallel Drizzle queries.

query.description: Embed two related tables. Each parenthesized clause is an embed with its own column projection.
EMBED FILTER — applies inside the embedded executive_compensation rows only. Format: `<embed>.<col>=<op>.<value>`.

item.name: Auth
item.description: Get a user JWT to access RLS-protected tables. The 'Sign in with password' request automatically writes the returned access_token into `{{user_jwt}}` via a test script.

exec: console.log('Saved user_jwt and user_id to environment');

item.name: Latest filing — raw_data only (single object)
item.description:
Returns just the `raw_data` jsonb of the most recently filed row for the given company. Uses `select=raw_data` to project only that one column, `order=filed_at.desc&limit=1` to take the latest, and the single-object Accept header so the response is the object — not a one-element array.

If you'd rather sort by DB row creation time, swap `order` to `created_at.desc`. Note the difference: `filed_at` is when SEC received the filing; `created_at` is when our row was inserted (could differ on backfills).

Requires `{{company_id}}` to be set in the environment.

header.description: Strip the array wrapper — return the row as an object. 406 if 0 or >1 rows match (limit=1 prevents the >1 case).

query.description: Project only this column. Massive payload reduction since `raw_data` already is the SEC response — `select=*` would also pull ai_summary, human_summary, etc.
Most recent SEC filing first. Alt: `created_at.desc` (DB row creation order).
Single row.
Optional filter — narrow to a specific filing type (e.g. 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, DEF 14A).

item.name: Latest filing — raw_data jsonb path (single field inside)
item.description: If you only need one field inside the `raw_data` jsonb blob, use the PostgREST jsonb arrow operators in the select. `->` returns jsonb, `->>` returns text. Replace `your_field` with the actual key.

query.description: Pull a sub-field of the raw_data jsonb. Use `->>` instead of `->` if you want it as a plain string rather than nested json. The `value:` prefix renames the column in the response.

item.name: List (limit 25, exclude vector column)
item.description: Embeddings store 1024-dim vectors — projecting them in JSON is wasteful. Exclude with explicit select.

item.name: List (metadata only — exclude payload)
item.description: raw_sec_responses contains verbatim SEC API JSON which can be huge. Exclude the payload column unless you need it.

item.name: users (RLS — needs user_jwt)
item.description: Requires authenticated session. Run Auth → Sign in with password first.

item.name: List (limited by RLS to your row)
Query Patterns (cookbook)
item.description: Copy-paste examples of PostgREST features you'll use across tables.

item.name: Embed FK relation
item.description: Bring back parent company alongside each director using FK relationship traversal.

item.name: Single object (404 if not exactly one row)
REFERENCE — every query parameter (all disabled, flip on what you need)
item.description:
Hit Send as-is and you get the full companies table (only `select=*` is enabled). Every other row is a disabled example of a PostgREST query parameter you can enable. Substitute real column names from the table you're targeting.

PostgREST docs: https://postgrest.org/en/stable/references/api/tables_views.html

query.description: PROJECTION — return all columns. Default if omitted.
PROJECTION — explicit column list. Cheaper than `*` for wide tables.
PROJECTION — alias columns: `output_name:db_column`.
PROJECTION — cast a column to another type using `column::type`.
PROJECTION — extract jsonb field as text. Use `->` for jsonb subobject, `->>` for text.
EMBED — pull the related directors rows for each company via FK relationship.
EMBED — relation with column projection on the embedded table.
EMBED — disambiguate which FK to follow when there are multiple FKs between the same two tables.
EMBED — multiple relations in one request.
EMBED — return only the count of related rows.
FILTER eq — equals.
FILTER neq — not equal.
FILTER gt — greater than.
FILTER gte — greater than or equal.
FILTER lt — less than.
FILTER lte — less than or equal.
FILTER like — SQL LIKE, case-sensitive. `*` is the wildcard (URL-friendly substitute for `%`).
FILTER ilike — case-insensitive LIKE. Most commonly used for substring search.
FILTER match — POSIX regex, case-sensitive.
FILTER imatch — POSIX regex, case-insensitive.
FILTER in — value in list. Comma-separated, parens required. Quote strings with commas: `in.("a,b",c)`.
FILTER is — IS NULL. Also: `is.true`, `is.false`, `is.unknown`.
FILTER isdistinct — IS DISTINCT FROM (null-safe inequality).
FILTER fts — full-text search using `to_tsquery`. Combine words with `&`, `|`, `!`.
FILTER plfts — plain full-text search via `plainto_tsquery` (no operator parsing).
FILTER phfts — phrase full-text search via `phraseto_tsquery`. Words must appear in order.
FILTER wfts — websearch FTS via `websearch_to_tsquery`. Google-style operators.
FILTER cs — contains. For arrays/jsonb/range. e.g. array contains `{apple,tech}`.
FILTER cd — contained by.
FILTER ov — overlap (any element in common).
FILTER sl — strictly left of (range type).
FILTER sr — strictly right of.
FILTER nxr — does not extend right.
FILTER nxl — does not extend left.
FILTER adj — adjacent to.
NEGATION — prefix any operator with `not.`. Works with ilike, in, is, etc.
NEGATION — `not.ilike` excludes substring matches.
LOGICAL or — comma-separated conditions inside parens. Equivalent to SQL OR.
LOGICAL and — explicit AND group. Multiple top-level params already AND together; this is mainly used inside `or`.
LOGICAL nested — combine `or` with nested `and(...)` for complex predicates.
LOGICAL nested with negation — `not.and(...)` inverts a sub-group.
ORDER — ascending by column. `asc` is default; can omit.
ORDER — descending.
ORDER — control null placement: `nullsfirst` or `nullslast`.
ORDER — multi-column, comma-separated. First column is primary sort.
PAGINATION — cap rows returned. Pair with `offset` or use the `Range` header.
PAGINATION — skip N rows. Combine with `limit`.
EMBED MODIFIER — order rows inside the embedded `directors(...)` relation.
EMBED MODIFIER — limit rows inside the embedded relation.
EMBED MODIFIER — offset inside the embedded relation.
EMBED MODIFIER — filter the embedded relation. Format: `<relation>.<col>=<op>.<value>`.
EMBED MODIFIER — alternate way to project columns on the embedded relation (also can be done inside the select itself).
WRITE-ONLY — for POST upserts: name the unique column to match on. Pair with `Prefer: resolution=merge-duplicates`.
WRITE-ONLY — for POST: limit which columns are inserted from the body.

item.name: REFERENCE — every header (all disabled, flip on what you need)
item.description: Companion to the query-param reference. Every PostgREST-aware header you might use, with descriptions.

header.description: AUTH — Supabase project key. Always required.
RESPONSE FORMAT — default. JSON array of rows.
RESPONSE FORMAT — single object instead of array. 406 if 0 or >1 rows match.
RESPONSE FORMAT — CSV output.
RESPONSE FORMAT — at the API root, returns the OpenAPI spec describing every table and RPC.
SCHEMA — read from a non-default Postgres schema (must be exposed via `db-schemas` setting).
SCHEMA — for writes: target a non-default schema.
COUNT — total row count returned in `Content-Range` response header. Slow on large tables.
COUNT — fast estimate from query planner. Cheap but approximate.
COUNT — exact when small, planned when large. Good default for unknown sizes.
WRITE — include the inserted/updated/deleted row(s) in the response body.
WRITE — don't return a body. Default for inserts.
WRITE — return only Location header pointing at the new row.
UPSERT — on conflict, UPDATE the existing row. Pair with `on_conflict=<col>` query param.
UPSERT — on conflict, leave existing row alone.
WRITE — when a column is missing from the body, use its DB default instead of NULL.
TRANSACTION — commit (default).
TRANSACTION — execute and roll back. Useful for dry-run testing of writes.
VALIDATION — error on unknown query parameters or headers. Default is `lenient`.
TIMEZONE — set the session timezone for this request (affects timestamp formatting).
PAGINATION — alternative to `limit`/`offset`. Inclusive 0-indexed range. Pair with `Prefer: count=exact` to get totals.
PAGINATION — must be `items` (the only supported unit).
CONCURRENCY — only act if the resource ETag matches (optimistic locking).
